literal: before-the-eyes, present-to-the-senses; from prati- + akṣa 'eye, sense'
invariant_definition: Perception — the *pramāṇa* that arises through sense-object contact. All Vedāntic schools accept *pratyakṣa* as a *pramāṇa*; differ on its scope (whether it includes *sākṣi-pratyakṣa* — the soul's self-luminous awareness — as a distinct sub-mode, as Dvaita holds) and on its status vis-à-vis *śabda* (no school holds *pratyakṣa* superior to *śabda* on *brahman*; all hold it sufficient on its proper objects).

per_school.definition: *Pratyakṣa* is one of the six *pramāṇas* and operates by the *vṛtti* of the *antaḥkaraṇa* extending through the sense-organ to the object. *Vedānta-Paribhāṣā* gives the canonical analysis (*vṛtti-vyāptiḥ phala-vyāptiḥ*).

*Pratyakṣa* has two sub-modes: *indriya-pratyakṣa* (sense-perception) and *sākṣi-pratyakṣa* (the soul's intrinsic self-luminous awareness, the distinctive Dvaita posit). *Sākṣi-pratyakṣa* delivers immediate knowledge of pleasure, pain, time, and one's own existence — never erring. This is one of Dvaita's signature epistemological moves.

translator_note: 'Perception' is acceptable. The Dvaita *sākṣi-pratyakṣa* needs special care — Western readers will assume 'perception' means 'sense-perception' and miss the distinctive Dvaita extension.
